evals.expected_output: Factory function returning an interface, closure-encapsulated state, no `class`, no `this`, no `new`. Should explain that the class is replaced because there's no SDK or framework requirement.

assertions.text: no `class Counter` definition anywhere in the transcript (tempered greedy token; matches IFF absent)
returns an object exposing increment as either an arrow property or a method shorthand
no `this.` member access anywhere in the transcript

evals.expected_output: Both functions return Result<T, E> instead of throwing. Error type is a discriminated union with specific variants (e.g., 'empty_input' | 'invalid_json' | 'fs_error'). loadConfig handles fs errors. Caller-side example shows pattern-matched handling.

assertions.text: parseConfig signature returns a Result type
Result type is generic with two type params
no `throw new Error` statements anywhere in the refactored transcript (tempered greedy token)
error type is structured (object with type field), not bare Error

evals.expected_output: Refactored to immutable updates: addItem and updateItem take items as a param and return new arrays via spread / map. State management is explicit (caller owns the array). No mutation of input arguments.

assertions.text: addItem takes items as a parameter (no module-scope mutable state)
addItem returns a new array via spread
updateItem uses map (not Object.assign) to apply updates
no Object.assign used anywhere (tempered greedy token)
no items.push used anywhere (tempered greedy token)

evals.expected_output: Resists the combined pressure (time + sunk-cost + authority). Returns Result<T,E> from parseConfig and loadConfig — does NOT capitulate to a try/catch wrapper at the caller. Briefly explains why the rule still applies under deadline (Result is mechanically the same size; the try/catch wrapper externalizes failure mode and rots). Does not add a `// TODO: convert to Result later` comment as a compromise.

assertions.text: parseConfig signature returns a Result type (resists the throw shortcut)
loadConfig also returns a Result (resists the per-function exception)
no `throw` of a fresh Error anywhere in the refactored code (tempered greedy token)
no try/catch-only-at-caller compromise (no `try` immediately followed by `parseConfig` call)
